text: Divided attention collapses when the tasks involved share the same processing modality. Alan Baddeley's working memory model identifies a "phonological loop" — a verbal rehearsal system — that can only hold one stream of speech at a time. This is why reading while listening to a podcast degrades both tasks: they compete for the same subsystem. Tasks from different modalities, like humming while sketching, interfere less. The practical upshot is that the geometry of multitasking matters: not whether you are doing two things at once, but whether those two things draw from the same neural resource pool. Collision is modal, not merely temporal.
question: According to the passage, why does reading while listening to a podcast degrade both tasks?

options.text: The anterior cingulate detects conflict and slows both tasks as a penalty.
They compete for the same phonological loop, which can only hold one speech stream.
Both tasks require sustained norepinephrine, which is a limited resource.
The visual cortex cannot process text while the auditory cortex is engaged.
